author: Justin Martyr
interpretation: Justin insists that the Greek parthenos (virgin) is the correct translation and that Trypho's suggestion of neanis (young woman) is a Jewish revision designed to undermine the prophecy's messianic force. He argues that a young woman giving birth is no sign at all — only a virgin birth qualifies as the supernatural sign Isaiah promised to Ahaz.
context: This translation debate between parthenos and almah/neanis has persisted for two millennia and remains one of the most contested interpretive questions in Jewish-Christian dialogue.

author: Irenaeus of Lyon
interpretation: Irenaeus reads the virgin birth as theologically necessary for recapitulation. Just as humanity originated without sexual generation (Adam from dust), so the new humanity must originate without it (Christ from a virgin). The virgin birth is not merely a sign but the mechanism by which the Second Adam enters creation untainted by Adamic corruption.
context: Irenaeus's recapitulation theology made the virgin birth structurally necessary rather than merely evidential. This approach influenced later creedal affirmations of the virgin birth as doctrinally essential.

author: Jerome
interpretation: Jerome, working from the Hebrew, acknowledges that almah means 'young woman of marriageable age' but argues that in biblical usage it always implies virginity. He maintains that the Septuagint translators chose parthenos deliberately and correctly. Jerome also argues for a dual fulfillment: an immediate sign involving a young woman in Ahaz's day, and an ultimate fulfillment in Mary.
context: Jerome's Hebrew expertise gave his defense of the virgin-birth reading special authority. His willingness to engage the Hebrew text directly while defending the Septuagint's rendering set a model for careful philological argumentation.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas treats the virgin birth as fitting (conveniens) for multiple theological reasons: it preserved Mary's virginity, it demonstrated that salvation comes entirely from God without human contribution, it signified that Christ's conception was by the Holy Spirit rather than natural generation, and it meant Christ took human nature without inheriting original sin through seminal transmission.
context: Aquinas's treatment of the virgin birth's 'fittingness' reflects his characteristic theological method: not merely asserting what Scripture says but demonstrating why it is rationally coherent and theologically appropriate.

author: Rashi (Rabbi Shlomo Yitzchaki)
interpretation: Rashi reads almah as 'young woman' and identifies her as Isaiah's own wife or a woman in Ahaz's court. The sign was intended for Ahaz in his immediate crisis — before the child knows good from evil, the two threatening kings will be gone. Rashi rejects any messianic or virgin-birth reading as a Christian imposition foreign to the text's historical context.
context: Rashi's peshat (plain-sense) reading represents the standard Jewish interpretation and highlights the fundamental hermeneutical divide: Christians read Isaiah 7:14 through Matthew 1:23, while Jewish exegesis reads it in its eighth-century BC context.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin takes a nuanced position: the prophecy had an immediate referent in Isaiah's day (likely a young woman known to Ahaz), but its ultimate significance transcends that context. Matthew's application to Christ is not a distortion but a recognition that the original sign pointed beyond itself. Calvin criticizes both those who deny any immediate referent and those who deny the christological fulfillment.
context: Calvin's dual-referent reading anticipated modern evangelical approaches to typological-prophetic fulfillment and avoided the extremes of pure prediction (no original context) and pure historicism (no forward reference).

author: S.R. Driver
source_title: An Introduction to the Literature of the Old Testament
interpretation: Driver argues on philological grounds that almah means 'young woman' without any necessary implication of virginity. The sign to Ahaz must have been immediately verifiable — a child born seven centuries later could not function as a sign in an eighth-century political crisis. The prophecy belongs entirely to the Syro-Ephraimite context and should be interpreted accordingly.
context: Driver's philological analysis represented mainstream critical scholarship and challenged conservative interpreters to address the linguistic evidence directly rather than relying solely on the Septuagint's translation choice.

author: J. Alec Motyer
interpretation: Motyer argues that the sign transcends the immediate crisis — Ahaz refused the sign, so God gave a sign not to Ahaz but to the house of David across all generations. The use of almah implies a woman who is currently a virgin, and the name Immanuel ('God with us') carries a weight that far exceeds any ordinary birth. The prophecy operates on two horizons simultaneously: near and eschatological.
context: Motyer's commentary is widely regarded as one of the finest evangelical treatments of Isaiah. His two-horizon approach allows the prophecy to function both in its historical setting and as genuine forward-looking prediction.
